transcript.clean_text: The east coast of Italy and Abruzzo was the backdrop and terrain for the opening stage of the 106th Giro d'Italia. 3 and 1/2 thousand kilometers roughly all the way to Rome. All roads leading to Rome on this year's Giro starting with a time trial over 70 kilometers against the clock this year. The most in a decade or so and beginning with 19.6 kilometers all along an old bike path along the Costa dei Trabocchi. Trabocchi, the old fisherman's huts that still line the coastline. Two time checks along the way and then a final two kilometer climb to Ortona after the race started in Fossacesia Marina. A difficult beginning by no means an easy prologue start to a grand tour this. The first grand tour of the year getting on the way with Stefan de Bod setting the fastest time before that was broken by a very quick Magnus Cort Nielsen. Some lightning speeds as a lot of the riders had a little bit of help from the wind on the coast. Averages well above 50 kilometers per hour. And that average was pushed up even more by Brandon McNulty. The young American rider impressive year on year in his short career to date and 22:06 was his top time. It would be his UAE Emirates teammate Jay Vine who would go even quicker though. The Australian national champion highly impressive since joining the team and not even a spring knee injury could get in the way of this top performance. Two seconds quicker and that average now above 53 K's an hour. Then Tao Geoghegan Hart turned up for business. The former Giro d'Italia winner, 2020 victory went to him. Just seven seconds slower than everyone else at the bottom but a really quick climb taking the lead and becoming the first man under 22 minutes. But the level will be pushed even higher. João Almeida, 15 days in the maglia rosa. One more day than Marco Pantani in total in his career, and it looked as though he's going to have a 16th. He crossed the line in 21:48. A great start for the Portuguese national champion, until Evenepoel, Roglič, Küng, and Ganna all turned up. The two GC favorites, the two TT favorites, two big jewels on the road, but in the end, it was never a competition. Remco Evenepoel, 20 seconds faster than anybody else at the first check, 26 in front of Primož Roglič. That translated to a faster time at the start of the final climb. Around half a minute on Almeida there, as Ganna behind was going well, but not quite well enough to take the victory off of Evenepoel. 15 seconds slower at the bottom of the final climb. Evenepoel at the same time was further up it and motoring on to victory. Almeida could only watch and appreciate what he was seeing. A demolition job by Remco Evenepoel. It's 40 years since a world road champion won the Giro. It's 33 years since a rider took the maglia rosa on the opening day and carried it all the way. But Evenepoel, well ahead of Roglič, well ahead of the rest, a candidate to do just that. Küng rode well. Ganna did his best. Roglič, there was nothing too much wrong with his ride, either. The problem for them was on the day there was simply not Remco Evenepoel in this form. No Top Ganna third film with the best actor performance for Ganna. All the glory, all the plaudits, the maglia rosa going to Remco Evenepoel. 10 seconds ahead of Ganna, 29 on Almeida, but look at that gap to Roglič, 43 seconds. A lot of time after one stage of a 21-day Grand Tour. Plenty of twists and turns, lots of ups and downs from Abruzzo to Rome, but a big, big super start for Remco Evenepoel. Stage two should be the first sprint opportunity. It's just over 200 km, mostly down the coast after a hilly start. Through Pescara, the obligatory visit to Chieti for this region as well. Intermediate sprint there before a finale in San Salvo Marina at the end of a long day on the road. The first road stage of the Giro d'Italia should be an opportunity for the sprinters. They'll be hoping to hold on to the end, and we hope you can join us. You can catch the Giro d'Italia live across Warner Bros. Discovery Sports and on GCN+ live and on demand each and every day.
